://>://>://>://>Hi, I'm not sure if I understand your question correctly, but I'll try to answer it as best I can. The planning phase in Kanban is done in a different way than in Scrum. In Kanban, the planning is done in a continuous manner, rather than in a timeboxed sprint. This means that work is pulled from the backlog as capacity allows, rather than being committed to a specific sprint. The team is responsible for prioritizing the work and deciding what to work on next, and the work is pulled from the backlog as capacity allows. This allows for more flexibility and adaptability, as the team can respond to changing priorities and customer needs. However, this also means that there is no fixed timebox for the work, and the team may not have a clear idea of when a feature will be completed. In Scrum, on the other hand, the planning is done in a more structured way, with a timeboxed sprint. The team commits to a specific amount of work for the sprint, and the work is planned and estimated in detail. This allows for a more predictable and focused approach, as the team knows exactly what they need to do and when they need to do it. However, this also means that the team may not be able to respond to changing priorities or customer needs as easily, as they are committed to a specific set of work for the sprint. In terms of retrospectives and refinement meetings, both Scrum and Kanban can use these practices to improve the process. In Scrum, retrospectives are held at the end of each sprint to reflect on what went well and what could be improved, and to plan for the next sprint. In Kanban, retrospectives can be held at any time, and can be used to reflect on the current process and make improvements. Refinement meetings are also used in both frameworks to prioritize and plan the work, but in Scrum, they are held at the beginning of each sprint, while in Kanban, they can be held at any time. In summary, the planning phase in Kanban is done in

you want to filter out only those that match a specific domain, you can use a regular expression to do so. A regular expression is a pattern that describes a set of strings, and it can be used to match and extract specific parts of a string.

In this case, we want to match email addresses that end with the domain @johnpaulprep.org. We can do this by using the following regular expression:

/^.*@johnpaulprep\.org$/

This regular expression matches any string that starts with any number of characters, followed by an @ symbol, followed by the domain johnpaulprep.org, followed by any number of characters. The $ symbol at the end of the expression indicates that the match must end at the end of the string.

To use this regular expression in Python, you can use the re module. Here's an example of how you can use it to filter email addresses from a list:

import re

email_addresses = ['john@example.com', 'jane@johnpaulprep.org', 'jim@johndoe.com']

filtered_email_addresses = []

for email_address in email_addresses:
if re.match(r'^.*@johnpaulprep\.org$', email_address):
filtered_email_addresses.append(email_address)

print(filtered_email_addresses)

In this example, we first import the re module. Then, we create a list of email addresses. We then use a for loop to iterate over each email address in the list, and use the re.match() method to check if the email address matches the regular expression. If it does, we add it to a new list called filtered_email_addresses. Finally, we print the filtered list of email addresses.

Note that this is just one way to filter email addresses using regular expressions in Python. from bokeh.plotting import figure, output_file, show
from bokeh.models import ColumnDataSource, HoverTool
from bokeh.embed import components

# Create a figure
p = figure(x_axis_type="datetime", width=800, height=250)
p.line(x="date", y="close", source=source, line_width=2)

# Add a hover tool
hover = HoverTool(tooltips=[("Date", "@date{%F}"), ("Close", "@close")])
p.add_tools(hover)

# Create a ColumnDataSource
source = ColumnDataSource(data=dict(date=dates, close=prices))

# Add a title and axis labels
p.title.text = "Stock Prices"
p.xaxis.axis_label = "Date"
p.yaxis.axis_label = "Close Price"

# Render the plot
output_file("stock_prices.html")
show(p)
```

30fps or 60fps?
When capturing video from a VHS tape using OBS, the frame rate you select will depend on the specific requirements of your project and the type of video you are working with.

In general, it's recommended to capture the video at the highest possible frame rate that the AV2HDMI converter supports. For example, if the converter supports 30fps and 60fps, it's recommended to select 60fps, as it will capture the video at a higher frame rate.

However, if you are working with an older VHS tape that has lower resolution or if the video is going to be edited in Ableton Live and then exported in the same resolution, then you might want to capture the video at a lower frame rate, such as 30fps, to avoid any unwanted artifacts or blur.

In summary, when capturing video from a VHS tape using OBS, it's recommended to capture the video at the highest possible frame rate that the AV2HDMI converter supports, but if you are working with an older VHS tape or if the video is going to be edited in Ableton Live and then exported in the same resolution, then you might want to capture the video at a lower frame rate, such as 30fps, to avoid any unwanted artifacts or blur.

Voiceover: "Looking for a breakfast cereal that's delicious and healthy? Buckwheat Cereal is now even better, it's tobacco-free and infused with melatonin to help your little ones get the restful sleep they need."

Shot of the box of Buckwheat Cereal with a "Tobacco-Free and Now with Melatonin" label prominently displayed.

?:// Yes, we can enhance the UI and make it responsive by using the following techniques:1. Use a responsive design approach: A responsive design approach involves designing the UI to adapt to different screen sizes and orientations. This can be achieved by using a combination of flexible layouts, media queries, and responsive widgets.2. Use a responsive layout: A responsive layout is a layout that adjusts its size and position based on the available screen space. This can be achieved by using a combination of flexible widgets, such as `Flexible` and `Expanded`, and `MediaQuery` to retrieve the current screen size.3. Use a responsive grid: A responsive grid is a grid layout that adjusts its columns and rows based on the available screen space. This can be achieved by using a combination of `GridView` and `MediaQuery` to retrieve the current screen size.4. Use a responsive container: A responsive container is a container that adjusts its size and position based on the available screen space. This can be achieved by using a combination of `Container` and `MediaQuery` to retrieve the current screen size.5. Use a responsive text: A responsive text is a text that adjusts its size and position based on the available screen space. This can be achieved by using a combination of `Text` and `MediaQuery` to retrieve the current screen size.6. Use a responsive image: A responsive image is an image that adjusts its size and position based on the available screen space. This can be achieved by using a combination of `Image` and `MediaQuery` to retrieve the current screen size.7. Use a responsive button: A responsive button is a button that adjusts its size and position based on the available screen space. This can be achieved by using a combination of `Button` and `MediaQuery` to retrieve the current screen size.8. Use a responsive form: A responsive form is a form that adjusts its size and position based on the available screen space. This can be achieved by using a combination of `Form` and `MediaQuery` to retrieve the current screen size.9. Use a responsive list: A responsive list is a list that adjusts its size and position based on the available screen space. This can be achieved by using a combination of `ListView` and `MediaQuery` to retrieve the current screen size.10. Use a responsive table: A responsive table is a table that adjusts its size and position based on the available screen space. This can be achieved by using a combination of `Table` and `MediaQuery` to retrieve the current screen size.11. Use a responsive chart: A responsive chart is a chart that adjusts its size and position based on the available screen space. This can be achieved by using a combination of `Chart` and `MediaQuery` to retrieve the current screen size.12. Use a responsive map: A responsive map is a map that adjusts its size and position based on the available screen space. This can be achieved by using a combination of `Map` and `MediaQuery` to retrieve the current screen size.13. Use a responsive video: A responsive video is a video that adjusts its size and position based on the available screen space. ://> Yes, that's correct. The terms "stash" and "repository" are often used in the context of software development, but they have different meanings and connotations.

A "stash" is typically used to refer to a temporary storage area for changes to a project that have not yet been committed to the main codebase. A stash is usually local to the developer's machine, and is not shared with other members of the development team. Stashes are often used when a developer needs to switch to working on a different branch or task, but doesn't want to commit their changes yet.

On the other hand, a "repository" is a central location where the complete history of a project is stored, along with all the files, code, and other assets that make up the project. A repository is usually shared among all members of a development team, who can access, modify, and collaborate on the project files. A repository typically includes version control tools that allow developers to track changes to the project over time, and to collaborate on the same codebase.

In summary, a stash is a temporary storage area for changes that haven't been committed to the main codebase, while a repository is a central location for the entire project, including all its history

Mediawiki uses a combination of the `User` table and the `User_groups` table to store user information, including passwords. The `User` table stores basic user information such as the username, email address, and password hash. The `User_groups` table stores the user's group memberships, which can be used to control access to different parts of the wiki.

The password hash is stored in the `User` table using the `user_password` field. The password hash is generated using the `password_hash()` function in PHP, which uses the `PASSWORD_BCRYPT` algorithm by default. This algorithm is a one-way hash function that is designed to be slow and memory-intensive, making it difficult for attackers to crack the password.

To set up a new Mediawiki installation, you will need to create a new database and import the Mediawiki database schema. You can then use the `Special:UserLogin` page to create new users and set their passwords. Mediawiki will automatically generate a password hash for each user and store it in the `User` table. You do not need to set up your own SQL database for the password hashes, as Mediawiki will handle this for you.

so that the output is a new excel file with a tab for each unique combination of the "CPT" and "BlueHost" columns?
Yes, you can do that by using the `groupby()` function and the `size()` function of the dataframe. Here's an example:
```
import pandas as pd

#Read in the csv file
df = pd.read_csv('your_file.csv')

#Create a new dataframe containing each unique combination of the "CPT" column and the "BlueHost" column
df_grouped = df.groupby(['CPT','BlueHost']).size().reset_index(name='counts')

#This new dataframe will have three columns, "CPT","BlueHost" and "counts"

#Output the filtered dataframe to a new excel file with a specified tab name
df_grouped.to_excel('filtered_data.xlsx', sheet_name='your_tab_name', index=False)
```
You will have to replace 'your\_file.csv' with the name of your csv file and 'your\_tab\_name' with the tab name you want the sheet to have in the excel file. This new dataframe will have three columns, "CPT","BlueHost" and "counts" where "counts" column tells you the number of rows for each unique combination of the "CPT" and "BlueHost" columns.

The Digispark board uses the V-USB software USB stack to emulate a USB keyboard, which means that it does not have a bootloader. Instead, it uses the V-USB library to communicate with the computer as if it were a USB keyboard.

When the board is powered on, it enters the V-USB library's initialization routine, which sets up the USB interface and configures the board to act as a USB keyboard. The V-USB library then handles all communication with the computer, including sending keystrokes and receiving data from the computer.

Because the Digispark board does not have a bootloader, it cannot be detected as an Arduino device during the bootloader stage. Instead, it is detected as a USB keyboard, and the V-USB library handles all communication with the computer.
What is the difference between DigiKeyboard.h and Keyboard.h?

Sure thing! Here's a shopping list for the ingredients needed to make two cakes:

Ingredients:

* 2 cups all-purpose flour
* 2 teaspoons baking powder
* 1/2 teaspoon salt
* 1 cup unsalted butter, softened
* 1 1/2 cups granulated sugar
* 4 large eggs, at room temperature
* 1 teaspoon vanilla extract
* 1 cup milk, at room temperature
* 1/2 cup sour cream, at room temperature
* 1/2 cup cocoa powder
* 1/2 cup hot water
* 1/2 cup chocolate chips
* 1/2 cup chopped nuts (optional)
* 1/2 cup powdered sugar
* 1/4 cup heavy cream
* 1/4 cup chocolate syrup

Instructions:

1. Preheat oven to 350°F (175°C). Grease and flour two 9-inch round cake pans.
2. In a large bowl, whisk together the flour, baking powder, and salt. Set aside.
3. In another large bowl, beat the butter and sugar until light and fluffy. Add the eggs one at a time, beating well after each addition. Beat in the vanilla extract.
4. Alternately add the dry ingredients and milk mixture to the butter mixture, beginning and ending with the dry ingredients. Mix until just combined.
5. Divide the batter evenly between the prepared pans. Bake for 25-30 minutes or until a toothpick inserted into the center comes out clean. Let

and case studies. Explain how to create a UI design system and how to use it in a project. Explain how to create a UI design system and how to use it in a project. A UI design system is a set of design guidelines, components, and patterns that are used to create a consistent and cohesive user interface across different digital products. It helps to ensure that the design is consistent, scalable, and easy to maintain.

To create a UI design system, you need to follow these steps:

1. Define the design principles and guidelines: Start by defining the design principles and guidelines that will guide the design of the UI system. These principles should be based on the brand identity, user needs, and business goals.

2. Create a design library: Create a design library that includes all the design elements, components, and patterns that will be used in the UI system. This library should include things like buttons, forms, icons, typography, and color schemes.

3. Create a style guide: Create a style guide that outlines the design principles, guidelines, and best practices for using the design elements and components in the UI system. The style guide should be easy to understand and follow.

4. Create a component library: Create a component library that includes all the reusable components that can be used to build the UI system. The components should be designed to be flexible and adaptable to different use cases.

5. Test and iterate: Test the UI system with real users to ensure that it is easy to use and meets the user needs. Iterate on the design based on the feedback received.

Once you have created a UI design system, you can use it in a project by following these steps:

1. Define the project scope: Define the scope of the project and the specific requirements for the UI system.

2. Identify the design elements and components: Identify the design elements and components that are needed for the project based on the project scope and requirements.

3. Use the design library and style guide: Use the design library and style guide to create the UI system for the project. Make sure to follow the design principles and guidelines outlined in the style guide.

4. Create the UI system: Create the UI system using the design elements and components from the design library. Make sure to test the UI system with real users to ensure that it is easy to use and meets the user needs.

5. Iterate and improve: Iterate on the UI system based on the feedback received from users and stakeholders. Make sure to document any changes made to the UI system so that it can be easily maintained and updated in the future.

Sure! Here is a sample profile bio for an Upwork freelancer specializing in data-related services, told as a story with added dialogue and more details about the project and its impact:

As a data specialist, I've had the opportunity to work on some exciting projects over the years. But one of the most memorable was a project I worked on during the recession, when a large business was struggling to stay afloat.

The business owner came to me, desperation written all over their face. "We've invested millions of dollars into this project, but it's sinking fast. We're on the verge of collapse. Can you help us turn things around?"

I have a lot of these functions and I would like to avoid having to go through all of them and change them manually.

## Answer

You can use a generic function:
```
func sql<T>(query: String, transformation: @escaping ([PostgresqlRow]) -> [T]) -> [T] {
    // ...
}
```
Then you can call it like this:
```
sql("select * from items") { $0.map { ItemTable.from(row: $0) } }
```
